nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef slopes, lagoons, reef crests, and mangrove-adjacent areas; most commonly found between 5 and 20 m depth, with records to approximately 40 m

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Supplemental feeding of micro-plankton, rotifers, or comparable micro-foods approximately twice per week has been documented to support growth and coloration. Target feeding via pipette or broadcast feeding during polyp extension is recommended. Calcium, strontium, and trace elements should be dosed consistently.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS and soft corals with spacing
avoidWith: corals placed in direct physical contact
aggressive LPS corals with long sweeper tentacles

summary: Pocillopora is often considered the most beginner-friendly SPS coral — generally forgiving compared to Acropora, rapid-growing, and widely available in colorful captive-bred frags. P. damicornis is the iconic species.
careNotes:
Note: Pocillopora is a genus of approximately 20+ described species commonly discussed at the genus level in the aquarium trade because most species share very similar husbandry requirements. The scientificName field is set to 'Pocillopora sp.' to reflect this trade convention. P. damicornis (Cauliflower Coral) is the most iconic and widely kept species, but P. verrucosa, P. meandrina, and captive-bred designer morphs (such as the WWC Rainbow Pocillopora) are also frequently encountered.

Pocillopora is often described as the most beginner-accessible SPS coral in the reef hobby. Unlike Acropora, established colonies have been reported to tolerate moderate parameter swings and less-than-perfect water chemistry, making them a frequently recommended entry point into the SPS world.

Key care requirements:
• Lighting: Moderate to high intensity is generally advised (approximately 100–300 PAR). New frags should be acclimated by starting them lower in the rockwork and slowly moving them upward over 2–4 weeks to avoid bleaching. The species appears more tolerant of a wider light range than Acropora.
• Flow: High, chaotic (non-laminar) water movement is advised. Strong flow helps prevent detritus accumulation in the colony's branching structure and reduces susceptibility to bacterial infection. Polyps should visibly sway without being overwhelmed.
• Acclimation challenge: LiveAquaria notes that Pocillopora 'can be difficult to acclimate to the aquarium, but once established, becomes quite hardy for an SPS coral.' New specimens may initially show polyp retraction or color loss — patience during the first 2–4 weeks is advised.
• Water chemistry: Calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium should be maintained consistently, as with all SPS corals. Pocillopora tends to be more forgiving of minor fluctuations than Acropora, but stable alkalinity (8–12 dKH) is still important. A two-part dosing system or calcium reactor is commonly used.
• Growth rate: Generally considered a rapid grower among SPS corals. Colonies can quickly encroach on neighboring corals — plan adequate spacing (at least 5–8 cm from neighboring colonies).
• Pest and disease susceptibility: New frags should be coral-dipped before introduction. Pocillopora is susceptible to rapid tissue necrosis (RTN) and bacterial infections under stress. The species has documented resistance to bleaching relative to other SPS, but elevated temperatures above 28–29°C can still trigger bleaching events.
• Aggression: Pocillopora tends to be relatively non-aggressive compared to other SPS, with short sweeper tentacles that extend primarily at night. However, adequate spacing from all neighbors is still recommended to accommodate colony growth.
• Color variants: Available in green, pink, purple, yellow, and multicolored designer morphs (e.g., 'Rainbow Pocillopora'). Some captive-bred designer morphs may be positioned at slightly lower light and flow than wild-type specimens.
• Reproduction in captivity: Easily propagated by fragging with scissors or bone cutters. The species also reproduces sexually via brooding (larvae released near the new moon), which distinguishes it biologically from Acropora's broadcast-spawning strategy. Wild populations have larvae viable for up to 100 days, facilitating wide dispersal.
• P. damicornis biology: This species interacts with the widest diversity of Symbiodinium (zooxanthellae) types among known coral species — approximately 30 strains — which may partly explain its relative environmental tolerance.
breedingNotes: Fragging (cutting) is the primary propagation method in captivity and is widely practiced. Colonies can be fragged with scissors or bone cutters; frags as small as 1/2 inch typically encrust and attach to frag plugs or rubble. Sexual reproduction occurs via brooding: eggs and sperm are fertilized internally and planula larvae are released in batches near the new moon. Released larvae can remain viable for up to 100 days. Deliberate captive sexual breeding is not commonly practiced in the hobby.

additional.name: LiveAquaria — Pink Cauliflower Pocillopora Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked all care parameters (temperature, pH, salinity, kH, lighting, flow, placement, care level, temperament, feeding). Confirmed identical values to the Green Cauliflower listing: 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, kH 8–12, High lighting, Strong flow, Middle to Top placement, Moderate care, Peaceful temperament, micro-plankton twice weekly.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Pocillopora damicornis
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Pocilloporidae, Order: Scleractinia), native range (East Africa, Red Sea, Japan, Indonesia, Australia, Hawaii, Easter Island, western Central America), depth range (0–40 m, most common 5–20 m), colony size (up to 30 cm), reproduction (brooding hermaphrodite, larvae viable ~100 days), and bleaching resistance.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Pocillopora (genus)
additional.notes: Used for genus-level taxonomy and common name confirmation (cauliflower corals, brush corals). Confirmed family Pocilloporidae and feeding behavior (polyp tentacle extension primarily at night).

additional.name: World Wide Corals — WWC Rainbow Pocillopora Coral
additional.notes: WWC describes this captive-bred designer morph as requiring 'medium light' and 'medium flow' and as 'an excellent choice for beginners.' This is slightly more conservative than LiveAquaria's High/Strong recommendations for wild-type specimens, likely reflecting the captive-bred morph's positioning lower in the tank. The genus-level care parameters follow LiveAquaria, which covers standard wild-type Pocillopora. The beginner-friendliness characterization is consistent across sources.
